author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew I. 1.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “The book of the generation of Jesus Christ, the Son of David, the Son of Abraham.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Behold a third discourse, and we have not yet made an end of the prefatory matter.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It was not then for nought that I said, It is the nature of these thoughts to have a great depth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Come, then, let us speak to-day what remains.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What is it then that is now required?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why Joseph’s genealogy is traced, who had no part in the birth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And one cause we have mentioned already; but it is necessary to mention likewise the other, that which is more mystical and secret than the first.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then is this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He would not that it should be manifest to the Jews, at the time of the birth, that Christ was born of a virgin.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, be not troubled at the strangeness of the saying.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is no statement of mine, but of our fathers, wonderful and illustrious men.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if He disguised many things from the first, calling Himself Son of Man, and hath not everywhere clearly unfolded to us even His equality with the Father; why dost thou wonder at His having for a time disguised this also, taking order as He was for a certain great and marvellous purpose?and would have condemned her for adultery.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if in regard to the other matters, for which they had frequent precedents likewise in the old dispensation, they were quite shameless in their obstinacy(for so, because He had cast out devils, they called Him possessed; and because He healed on the Sabbath day, they supposed Him to be an adversary of God; and yet oftentimes even before this had the Sabbath been broken), what would they not have said, if this had been told them?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Especially asthey had all time before this on their side, in that it never had produced any such thing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if after so many miracles they still called Him son of Joseph, how before the miracles would they have believed that He was born of a virgin?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It is then for this reason that both Joseph has his genealogy traced, and the Virgin betrothed to him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if even he, who was both a just and wondrous man, required many things, in order that he should receive that which had come to pass; an angel, and the vision in dreams, and the testimony from the prophets; how could the Jews, being both dull and depraved, and of so unfriendly spirit towards Him, have admitted this idea into their minds?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the strangeness and novelty thereof would be sure greatly to disturb them, and the fact that they had never so much as heard of such a thing having happened in the times of their forefathers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as the man who was once persuaded that He is Son of God, would after that have no cause to doubt concerning this too; so he who was accounting Him to be a deceiver and an adversary of God, how could he but have been yet more offended by this, and have been led on unto the oppositenotion?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause neither do the apostles at the first directly say this, but while of His resurrection they discourse much and often (forasmuch as of this there were examples in the times before, although not such as this); that He was born of a virgin they do not say always: nay, not even His mother herself ventured to utter this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See, for instance, what saith the Virgin even to Himself: “Behold, Thy father and I have sought Thee.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if this suspicion had been entertained, neither would He any longer have been accounted to be a Son of David, and this opinion not being held, many other evils besides would have arisen.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause neither do the angels say these things to all, but to Mary only, and Joseph; but when showing to the shepherds the glad tidings of that which was come to pass, they no longer added this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But why is it, that having mentioned Abraham, and having said that “he begat Isaac, and Isaac, Jacob;” and not having made any mention of his brother; when he is come to Jacob, he remembers both “Judah, and his brethren”?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now there are some that say, it was because of the perverseness of Esau, and of the rest that came before.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But I should not say this; for if it were so, how is it that he a little after mentions such women?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It being out of contraries, in this place, that His glory is manifested; not by having great forefathers, but low and of little account.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to the lofty One it is a great glory to be able to abase Himself exceedingly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore then did He not mention them?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because Saracens, and Ishmaelites, and Arabians, and as many as are sprung from those ancestors, have nothing in common with the race of the Israelites.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause then he passes over those in silence, and hastens on to His forefathers, and those of the Jewish people.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore he saith, “And Jacob begat Judas and his brethren.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For at this point the race of the Jews begins to have its peculiar mark.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And Judas begat Phares and Zara of Thamar.”“What doest thou, O man, putting us in remembrance of a history that contains an unlawful intercourse?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But why is this said?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since, if we were recounting the race of a mere man, one might naturally have been silent touching these things; but if of God Incarnate, so far from being silent, one ought to make a glory of them, showing forth His tender care, and His power.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, it was for this cause He came, not to escape our disgraces, but to bear them away.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore as He is the more admired, in that He not only died, but was even crucified (though the thing be opprobrious, yet the more opprobrious the more doth it show Him full of love to man), so likewise may we speak touching His birth; it is not only because He took flesh upon Him, and became man, that we justly stand amazed at Him, but because He vouchsafed to have also such kinsfolk, being in no respect ashamed of our evils.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this He was proclaiming from the very beginnings of His birth, that He is ashamed of none of those things that belong to us; while He teaches us also hereby, never to hide our face at our forefathers’ wickedness, but to seek after one thing alone, even virtue.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For such a man, though he have an alien for his ancestor, though he have a mother who is a prostitute, or what you will, can take no hurt thereby.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if the whoremonger himself, being changed, is nothing disgraced by his former life, much more will the wickedness of his ancestry have no power to bring to shame him that is sprung of an harlot or an adulteress, if he be virtuous.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But he did these things not only to instruct us, but also to bring down the haughtiness of the Jews.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For since they, negligent about virtue in their own souls, were parading the name of Abraham,thinking they had for a plea their forefathers’ virtue; he shows from the very beginning that it is not in these things men ought to glory, but in their own good deeds.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Besides this, he is establishing another point also, to show that all are under sin, even their forefathers themselves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: At least their patriarch and namesake is shown to have committed no small sin, for Thamar stands against him, to accuse his whoredom.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And David too had Solomon by the wife whom he corrupted.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if by the great ones the law was not fulfilled, much more by the less.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if it was not fulfilled, all have sinned, and Christ’s coming is become necessary.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause he made mention also of the twelve patriarchs, by this again bringing down their pride at the noble birth of their fathers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because many of these also were born of women that were slaves; but nevertheless the difference of the parents did not make a difference in the children.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For all were equally both patriarchs and heads of tribes.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this is the precedence of the Church, this the prerogative of the nobility that is among us, taking its type from the beginning.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that whether thou be bond or free, thou hast from thence nothing more nor less; but the question is all about one thing only, namely, the mind, and the disposition of the soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But besides what we have said, there is another cause also, wherefore he hath mentioned even this history; for to be sure, Zara’s name was not cast at random on that of Phares.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: (For indeed it was irrelevant, and superfluous, when he had mentioned Phares, from whom he was to trace Christ’s genealogy, to mention Zara also.)

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore then did he mention him?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When Thamar was on the point of giving birth to them, the pangs having come upon her, Zara put forth his hand first.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then the midwife, when she saw this, in order that the first should be known, bound his hand with scarlet; but the child, when he was bound, drew in his hand, and when he had drawn it in, Phares came forth first, and then Zara.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The midwife when she saw this said, “Why was the hedge broken up for thee?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou the dark expression of mysteries?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it was not without purpose that these things were recorded for us: since neither was it worth our study to learn, what it might be that the midwife said; nor worth a narrative to know, that he who came out second, put forth his hand first.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then is the mysterious lesson?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: First, from the name of the childwe learn what is inquired, for Phares is “a division,” and “a breach.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And moreover from the thing itself, which took place; for it was not in the order of nature that, having thrust out his hand, he should draw it in again when bound; these thing neither belonged to a movement directed by reason, nor did they take place in the way of natural consequence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For after the hand had found its way out, that another child should come forth before was perhaps not unnatural; but that he should draw it back, and give a passage for another, was no longer after the manner of children at the birth, but the grace of God was present with the children, ordering these things, and sketching out for us by them a sort of image of the things that were to come.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Some of those who have examined these things accurately say, that these children are a type of the two nations.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And so in order that thou mightest learn that the polity of the latter people shone forth previously to the origin of the former, the child that hath the hand stretched forth doth not show itself entire, but draws even it in again; and after his brother had glided forth whole, then he too appears entire.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this took place also with regard to the two nations.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I mean, that after the polity of the Church had been manifested in the times of Abraham, and then had been withdrawn in the midst of its course, the Jewish people came, and the legal polity, and then the new people appeared entire with their own laws.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also the midwife saith, “Why was the hedge broken up for thee?” because the law coming in had broken in upon the freedom of the polity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed the Scripture is ever wont to call the law a hedge; as the prophet saith: “Thou hast broken down her hedge, so that all they which pass by the way do pluck off her grapes:”and, “I have set a hedge about it:”and Paul, “Having broken down the middle wall of the hedge.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But others say, that the saying, “Why was the hedge broken up for thee?” was spoken touching the new people: for this at its coming put down the law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou that it was not for few nor small causes that he brought to our remembrance the whole history concerning Judah?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this end he hath mentioned Ruth also and Rahab, the one an alien, the other an harlot, that thou mayest learn that He came to do away with all our ills.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He hath come as a Physician, not as a Judge.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore in like manner as those of old took harlots for wives, even so God too espoused unto Himself the nature which had played the harlot: and this also prophetsfrom the beginning declare to have taken place with respect to the Synagogue.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But that spouse was ungrateful towards Him who had been an husband to her, whereas, the Church, when once delivered from the evils received from our fathers, continued to embrace the Bridegroom.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See, for instance, what befell Ruth, how like it is to the things which belong to us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For she was both of a strange race, and reduced to the utmost poverty, yet Boaz when he saw her neither despised her poverty nor abhorred her mean birth, as Christ having received the Church, being both an alien and in much poverty, took her to be partaker of the great blessings.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But even as Ruth, if she had not before left her father, and renounced household and race, country and kindred, would not have attained unto this alliance; so the Church too, having forsaken the customs which men had received from their fathers, then, and not before,became lovely to the Bridegroom.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Of this therefore the prophet discourses unto her, and saith, “Forget thy people, and thy father’s house, so shall the king have pleasure in thy beauty.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This Ruth did too, and because of this she became a mother of kings, even as the Church did likewise.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For of her David himself sprung.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So then to shame them by all these things, and to prevail on them not to be high-minded, he hath both composed the genealogy, and brought forward these women.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yes, for this last, through those who intervened, was parent to the great king, and of these David is not ashamed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it cannot, nay, it cannot be that a man should be good or bad, obscure or glorious, either by the virtue or by the vice of his forefathers; but if one must say somewhat even paradoxical, he shines forth the more, who not being of worthy ancestors, has yet become excellent.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let no one therefore be high-minded on account of these matters, but let him consider the forefathers of the Lord, and put away all his haughtiness, and let good actions be his pride; or rather, not even these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For thus it was that the Pharisee came to be inferior to the Publican.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, if thou wouldest show the good work to be great, have no high thought,and thou hast proved it so much the greater.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Make account that thou hast done nothing, and then thou hast done all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if, being sinners, when we account ourselves to be what we are, we become righteous, as indeed the Publican did; how much more, when being righteous we account ourselves to be sinners.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since if out of sinners men are made righteous by a lowly mind (although this were not to be lowly-minded but to be right-minded); if then to be right-minded avails so much in the case of sinners, consider what will not lowliness of mind do with respect to righteous men.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Do not then mar thy labors, nor cast away from thee the fruits of thy toils, neither run thou in vain, making frustrate all thy labor after the many courses thou hast run.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, for thy Lord knows thy good works better than thou dost.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Though thou give but a cup of cold water, not even this doth He overlook; though thou contribute but a farthing, though thou shouldest utter a sigh only, He receives it all with great favor and is mindful thereof, and assigns for it great rewards.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But wherefore dost thou search out thine own doings, and bring them out before us continually?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Knowest thou not, that if thou praise thyself, God will no more praise thee? even as if thou bewail thyself, He will not cease proclaiming thee before all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is not at all His will that thy labors should be disparaged.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why do I say, disparaged?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, He is doing and contriving all things, so that even for little He may crown thee; and He goes about seeking excuses, whereby thou mayest be delivered from hell.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause, though thou shouldest work but the eleventh hour of the day, He gives thy wages entire; and though thou afford no ground of salvation, He saith, “I do it for mine own sake, that my name be not profaned:”though thou shouldest sigh only, though thou shouldest only weep, all these things He quickly catches hold of, for an occasion of saving thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us not therefore lift up ourselves, but let us declare ourselves unprofitable, that we may become profitable.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if thou call thyself approved, thou art become unprofitable, though thou wert approved; but if useless, thou art become profitable, even though thou wert reprobate.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore it is necessary to forget our good actions.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Yet how is it possible,” one may say, “not to know these things with which we are well acquainted?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How sayest thou?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Offending thy Lord perpetually, thou livest delicately, and laughest, and dost not so much as know that thou hast sinned, but hast consigned all to oblivion; and of thy good actions canst thou not put away the memory?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet fear is a stronger kind of thing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But we do the very contrary; on the one hand, whilst each day we are offending, we do not so much as put it before our mind; on the other, if we give a little money to a poor person, this we are ever revolving.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This kind of conduct comes of utter madness, and it is a very great loss to him who so makes his reckoning.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the secure storehouse of good works is to forget our good works.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And as with regard to raiment and gold, when we expose them in a market-place, we attract many ill-meaning persons; but if we put them by at home and hide them, we shall deposit them all in security: even so with respect to our good deeds; if we are continually keeping them in memory, we provoke the Lord, we arm the enemy, we invite him to steal them away; but if no one know of them, besides Him who alone ought to know, they will lie in safety.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Be not therefore for ever parading them, lest some one should take them away.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As was the case with the Pharisee, for bearing them about upon his lips; whence also the devil caught them away.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet it was with thanksgiving he made mention of them, and referred the whole to God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But not even did this suffice Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is not thanksgiving to revile others, to be vainglorious before many, to exalt one’s self against them that have offended.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Rather, if thou art giving thanks to God, be content with Him only, and publish it not unto men, neither condemn thy neighbor; for this is not thanksgiving.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wouldest thou learn words of thanksgiving? hearken unto the Three Children, saying, “We have sinned, we have transgressed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thou art righteous, O Lord, in all that thou hast done unto us, because thou hast brought all things upon us by a true judgment.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to confessone’s own sins, this is to give thanks with confessionunto God: a kind of thing which implies one to be guilty of numberless offenses, yet not to have the due penalty exacted.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This man most of all is the giver of thanks.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us beware therefore of saying anything about ourselves, for this renders us both odious with men and abominable to God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this reason, the greater the good works we do, the less let us say of ourselves; this being the way to reap the greatest glory both with men and with God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Or rather, not only glory from God, but a reward, yea, a great recompense.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Demand not therefore a reward that thou mayest receive a reward.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Confess thyself to be saved by grace, that He may profess Himself a debtor to thee; and not for thy good works only, but also for such rightness of mind.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when we do good works, we have Him debtor for our good works only; but when we do not so much as think we have done any good work, then also for this disposition itself; and more for this, than for the other things: so that this is equivalent to our good works.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For should this be absent, neither will they appear great.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in the same way, we too, when we have servants,do then most approve them when, after having performed all their service with good will, they do not think they have done anything great.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore, if thou wouldest make thy good deeds great, do not think them to be great, and then they will be great.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It was in this way that the centurion also said, “I am not fit that thou shouldest enter under my roof;” because of this, he became worthy, and was “marvelled at”above all Jews.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: On this wise again Paul saith, “I am not meet to be called an apostle;”because of this he became even first of all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So likewise John: “I am not meet to loose the latchet of His shoe;”because of this he was the “friend of the Bridegroom,” and the hand which he affirmed to be unworthy to touch His shoes, this did Christ draw unto His own head.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So Peter too said, “Depart from me, for I am a sinful man;”because of this he became a foundation of the Church.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For nothing is so acceptable to God as to number one’s self with the last.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This is a first principle of all practical wisdom.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he that is humbled, and bruised in heart, will not be vainglorious, will not be wrathful, will not envy his neighbor, will not harbor any other passion.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither when a hand is bruised, though we strive ten thousand times, shall we be able to lift it up on high.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If therefore we were thus to bruise our heartlikewise, though it were stirred by ten thousand swelling passions, it could not be lifted up, no, not ever so little.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if a man, by mourning for things pertaining to this life, drives out all the diseases of his soul, much more will he, who mourns for sins, enjoy the blessing of self-restraint.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But who,” one may say, “will be able thus to bruise his own heart?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Listen to David, who became illustrious chiefly because of this, and see the contrition of his soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How after ten thousand good works, and when he was on the point of being deprived of country, and home, and life itself, at the very season of his calamity, seeing a vile and outcast common soldier trample on the turn of his fortunesand revile him; so far from reviling him again, he utterly forbad one of his captains, who was desirous to have slain him, saying, “Let him alone, for the Lord hath bidden him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And again, when the priests desired to carry about the ark of Godwith him, he did not permit it; but what doth he say?“Let me set it down in the temple, and if God deliver me from the dangers that are before me, I shall see the beauty thereof; but if He say to me, I have no delight in thee, behold, here am I, let Him do to me as seemeth good unto Him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And that which was done with regard to Saul, again and again, even oftentimes, what excellence of self-restraint doth it not show?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, for he even surpassed the old law, and came near to the apostolic injunctions.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause he bore with contentedness all that came from the Lord’s hands; not contending against what befell him, but aiming at one object alone, namely, in everything to obey, and follow the laws set by Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And when after so many noble deeds on his part, he saw the tyrant, the parricide, the murderer of his own brother, that injurious, that frenzied one, possessing in his stead his own kingdom, not even so was he offended.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But “if this please God,” saith he, “that I should be chased, and wander, and flee, and that he should be in honor, I acquiesce, and accept it, and do thank God for His many afflictions.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not like many of the shameless and impudent ones, who when they have not done, no not the least part of his good works, yet if they see any in prosperity, and themselves enduring a little discouragement, ruin their own souls by ten thousand blasphemies.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But David was not such an one; rather he showed forth all modesty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also God said, “I have found David, the son of Jesse, a man after mine own heart.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Such a spirit as this let us too acquire, and whatever we may suffer we shall bear it easily, and before the Kingdom, we shall reap here the gain accruing from lowliness of mind.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus “learn,” saith He, “of me, for I am meek and lowly in heart, and ye shall find rest unto your souls.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore in order that we may enjoy rest both here and hereafter, let us with great diligence implant in our souls the mother of all things that are good, I mean humility.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For thus we shall be enabled both to pass over the sea of this life without waves, and to end our voyage in that calm harbor; by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, to whom be glory and might for ever and ever.
